q: The CEO tells the Product Owner that feature X must be at the top of the Product Backlog for the next release, overriding the PO's value ordering. The Product Owner should:
options: Comply — the CEO has ultimate authority over product decisions
Move X to the top and quietly deprioritize it later
Discuss with the CEO: present the value reasoning behind the current ordering, incorporate the CEO's perspective, and decide based on value
Ask the Scrum Master to mediate the disagreement
why: For the PO to succeed, the organization must respect the PO's decisions — but respect is earned through transparent reasoning, not stubbornness. The PO remains accountable for ordering by value, incorporates new perspectives into that reasoning, and decides. Quiet resistance violates transparency. The Scrum Master does not mediate PO ordering decisions.

q: Velocity has been high for six Sprints. Customer satisfaction and product usage metrics are flat or declining. The Product Owner should:
options: Celebrate the high velocity — the team is clearly productive
Ask the Developers to ship even more items per Sprint
Refocus the team and organization on outcome measures (customer impact, usage, value) rather than output
Pause releases until value rebounds
why: Velocity measures throughput, not value. A product shipping lots of features while customer outcomes decline is a feature factory — maximizing output at the expense of value. The PO is accountable for value. The fix is reorienting what the team and organization measure and discuss, not cutting throughput or pushing more output.

q: A feature has been in development for three Sprints. Recent customer interviews reveal users don't actually want it. The Product Owner has spent significant political capital getting it prioritized. What should the PO do?
options: Complete the feature to protect the PO's credibility
Complete it so the investment isn't fully wasted
Stop work, remove it from the backlog, and transparently communicate the reasoning to stakeholders
Scope it down so at least some version ships
why: Under empiricism, new evidence changes decisions. Sunk cost isn't a reason to continue delivering work that new evidence shows won't create value. Transparent pivots — with the reasoning — build long-term credibility far better than shipping a feature the PO privately knows won't help users. Scoping down still invests effort in the wrong direction.

q: An executive demands the Product Owner commit to a fixed release date six months out. Empirical data shows high variability in team output and significant remaining product uncertainty. The PO should:
options: Commit to the date so the organization can plan around it
Refuse — the PO cannot commit to any date because Scrum doesn't allow it
Share an empirical forecast with its confidence range and update it as learning occurs
Pad the forecast with a 50% buffer and commit to that date
why: Empirical forecasting under uncertainty produces ranges, not dates. Treating a forecast as a fixed commitment hides variability and manufactures false certainty. The PO shares the forecast honestly, updates as data evolves, and works with stakeholders to make decisions as learning accrues. Padded commitments still fail when variance exceeds the pad, and the pad itself hides information.

q: A competitor ships a feature your product lacks. Leadership demands you copy it immediately. How should an evidence-based Product Owner respond?
options: Prioritize copying the feature to stay competitive
Ignore the competitor and stay focused on the current Product Goal
Investigate whether the feature would deliver value for your users; decide based on evidence, not reaction
Run a small experiment to release a version of it quickly, then measure outcomes
why: A competitor's move is a signal, not a decision. An evidence-based PO evaluates whether copying fits *your* users and Product Goal before acting. Reactive copying often ships features that don't fit. Option 3 skips the evaluation and jumps to delivery; option 1 is purely reactive; option 2 uses the signal as one input to a value-driven decision.

q: An organization is adding three new Scrum Teams to a product that currently has one team. Which of the following are consistent with Scrum? (Select all that apply)
options: Each team has its own Product Owner
All teams share one Product Owner
All teams share one Product Backlog
All teams share one Product Goal
Each team maintains its own Definition of Done, independent of the others
why: At scale, Scrum preserves single accountability on the product: one Product Owner, one Product Backlog, one Product Goal. Teams contributing to the same product must share a consistent Definition of Done so Increments integrate. Multiple POs fragment value ordering; independent DoDs break Increment transparency at the product level.

q: Mid-Sprint, a major customer signals a shift that makes the current Sprint Goal obsolete. What is within the Product Owner's accountability?
options: Direct the Developers to immediately switch to new work
Cancel the Sprint and start a new one aligned to the new direction
Renegotiate the Sprint Backlog with the Developers to reshape the Sprint mid-flight
Wait for the Sprint Review to adapt the Product Backlog
why: Only the Product Owner has authority to cancel a Sprint, and an obsolete Sprint Goal is exactly the case where cancellation is right — it stops further waste on irrelevant work. Directing Developers ignores self-management; renegotiating scope is for changes within the Sprint Goal, not when the Goal itself is obsolete; waiting until Sprint Review extends the waste by days or weeks.

q: The Product Owner chooses to hide certain Product Backlog items from stakeholders to reduce noisy feedback and political pressure.
options: True — this is within the PO's discretion
False — this breaks the Product Backlog's transparency
why: The Product Backlog must be transparent to enable empirical decisions across the Scrum Team and stakeholders. Hiding items to manage stakeholder reactions trades short-term peace for long-term erosion of trust and empirical validity. If certain conversations are noisy, the fix is how the PO structures the engagement — not opacity.

q: The Developers want to invest a Sprint in reducing technical debt. Stakeholders want new features. The Product Owner should:
options: Side with stakeholders — new features are visible value
Side with the Developers — technical health matters
Treat debt reduction as part of the same value conversation: weigh reduced Ability to Innovate against near-term value and order accordingly
Alternate Sprints — one of features, one of debt
why: Technical debt erodes the organization's Ability to Innovate — an EBM Key Value Area — but not every piece of debt reduction is the highest-value option. The PO evaluates whether *this* debt work outranks *these* alternative items. Alternating Sprints ignores the actual trade-offs; reflexively siding with either group abdicates the PO's value-ordering accountability.

q: The Product Owner has spent months personally writing every Product Backlog item, while Developers have no direct contact with stakeholders. The Scrum Master could help by:
options: Taking over backlog item writing to reduce the PO's burden
Coaching the PO and Developers on direct stakeholder collaboration, and the organization on why it matters
Letting the arrangement continue — it's within the PO's discretion
Appointing a business analyst to handle stakeholder communication
why: The PO's accountability is maximizing value, not producing specifications. Direct collaboration between Developers and stakeholders is foundational to empiricism. The SM serves the Scrum Team and the organization by coaching this change. Taking over specification writing perpetuates the anti-pattern; a business analyst introduces a non-Scrum role; doing nothing ignores a clear dysfunction.
